title: HIV/AIDS: A Systematic Approach
objectives: Identify key diagnostic features of HIV/AIDS
Apply clinical reasoning to HIV/AIDS management

sections.title: Overview of HIV/AIDS
sections.content:
**HIV/AIDS**

Screening, CD4 count staging, opportunistic infections, ART principles, PrEP/PEP

**Key Points:**
• Screen: 4th gen Ag/Ab combo test (p24 antigen + HIV-1/2 antibodies)
• CD4 <200: AIDS definition; opportunistic infection prophylaxis thresholds
• CD4 <200: PCP prophylaxis (TMP-SMX); <100: Toxoplasma prophylaxis; <50: MAC prophylaxis (azithromycin)

sections.title: Pathophysiology & Key Features
sections.content:
• Screen: 4th gen Ag/Ab combo test (p24 antigen + HIV-1/2 antibodies)
• CD4 <200: AIDS definition; opportunistic infection prophylaxis thresholds

sections.title: Diagnosis & Management
sections.content:
• CD4 <200: PCP prophylaxis (TMP-SMX); <100: Toxoplasma prophylaxis; <50: MAC prophylaxis (azithromycin)
• ART for ALL patients regardless of CD4 count (treat early)
• PrEP (tenofovir/emtricitabine): for high-risk HIV-negative individuals; PEP: within 72 hours of exposure

sections.title: Clinical Pearls
sections.content:
**High-Yield Points for HIV/AIDS:**

• CD4 <200: PCP prophylaxis (TMP-SMX); <100: Toxoplasma prophylaxis; <50: MAC prophylaxis (azithromycin)
• ART for ALL patients regardless of CD4 count (treat early)
• PrEP (tenofovir/emtricitabine): for high-risk HIV-negative individuals; PEP: within 72 hours of exposure

mcqs.question: Which of the following is MOST characteristic of HIV/AIDS?

options.text: Screen: 4th gen Ag/Ab combo test (p24 antigen + HIV-1/2 antibodies)
options.explanation: Screen: 4th gen Ag/Ab combo test (p24 antigen + HIV-1/2 antibodies)

options.text: Requires invasive testing in all cases

mcqs.question: What is the KEY diagnostic or management consideration for HIV/AIDS?

options.text: PrEP (tenofovir/emtricitabine): for high-risk HIV-negative individuals; PEP: within 72 hours of exposure
options.explanation: PrEP (tenofovir/emtricitabine): for high-risk HIV-negative individuals; PEP: within 72 hours of exposure

options.text: Immediate surgical intervention in all cases

mcqs.question: A patient presents with findings consistent with HIV/AIDS. Which finding would you expect?

options.text: CD4 <200: AIDS definition; opportunistic infection prophylaxis thresholds
options.explanation: CD4 <200: AIDS definition; opportunistic infection prophylaxis thresholds

mcqs.stem: A patient with HIV has a CD4 count of 75 cells/μL and presents with subacute headache, fever, and confusion. India ink stain of CSF is positive. What is the recommended initial treatment?

options.text: IV amphotericin B deoxycholate + flucytosine for 2 weeks (induction), then fluconazole
options.explanation: Positive India ink indicates Cryptococcus neoformans meningitis. Per IDSA/WHO guidelines, induction therapy is amphotericin B + flucytosine × 2 weeks, followed by consolidation with fluconazole 400mg/day × 8 weeks, then maintenance fluconazole 200mg/day. Do NOT start ART immediately — wait 4-6 weeks to avoid immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS).
Fluconazole monotherapy for induction has significantly higher mortality than amphotericin-based regimens. It is used for consolidation and maintenance, not induction.

options.text: IV ceftriaxone + vancomycin for bacterial meningitis coverage
options.explanation: The India ink positivity confirms cryptococcal meningitis. Bacterial meningitis coverage is not indicated when the organism is identified. Antifungal therapy is required.

options.text: Start ART immediately along with amphotericin B
options.explanation: Starting ART simultaneously with antifungal induction significantly increases risk of IRIS, which has high mortality in cryptococcal meningitis. ART should be deferred 4-6 weeks per guidelines.
